Pardon Industry Offers Rich Offenders a Path to Trump
When President Trump pardoned Joseph Schwartz, a former nursing-home owner, in November, I was curious about the back story and began investigating.
With some of Trump’s clemency grants, political or personal connections to him or his team are immediately clear. That was not the case with Schwartz.
Instead, I learned, Schwartz had paid a number of lawyers and lobbyists who had ties to Trump’s orbit to try to secure freedom.

When he was released from the minimum-security prison camp in Otisville, N.Y., after serving only three months of a three-year sentence, other inmates began discussing whom they could pay to achieve the same result.

Karoline Leavitt, the White House press secretary, dismissed the notion that lobbyists had shaped Trump’s clemency decisions. “Anyone spending money to lobby for pardons is foolishly wasting their money,” she said in a statement.
